lesson.title: Pointers
lesson.summary: Reference values by memory address — understand & and *.

sections.heading: What is a pointer
sections.bodyHtml:
Every value in your program lives somewhere in memory. A pointer holds the memory address of another value — it “points to” that value.

Use & to get the address of a variable, and * to get the value at an address (dereferencing):

x := 42
p := &x          // p is a pointer to x (type *int)
fmt.Println(p)   // prints something like 0xc000018030
fmt.Println(*p)  // 42 — follow the pointer

The type *int means “pointer to an int”. Every type has a pointer version: *string, *bool, and so on.

Think of & as “where does this live?” and * as “what is actually there?”.

sections.heading: Using pointers safely
sections.bodyHtml:
The zero value of any pointer is nil — it points to nothing. Dereferencing a nil pointer crashes your program, so always check first:

var p *int         // nil
if p != nil {
    fmt.Println(*p)
}

Pointers let functions modify the caller’s original value. Without a pointer, Go makes a copy:

func zeroVal(v int) {
    v = 0            // changes the copy only
}
func zeroPtr(p *int) {
    *p = 0           // changes the original
}

Pass a pointer when you need to mutate the original or when the value is large and copying would be expensive.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/pointers/main.go.

Write a function double(v *int) that multiplies the value at the pointer by 2. Call it from main and print the result.

Gets the value at a memory address
